texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.15 WHAT THIS CHAPTER MEANS FOR YOUR DEVELOPMENT TEAM
texts.text: 4.15 WHAT THIS CHAPTER MEANS FOR YOUR DEVELOPMENT TEAM

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Your development team must work together to elicit,understand,and document requirements. Often, different team members concentrate on separate aspects of the requirements: the  networking  expert  may  work  on  network  requirements, the  user-interface expert on screens and reports,the database expert on data capture and storage,and so on. Because  the  disparate  requirements  will  be  integrated  into  a  comprehensive whole, requirements must be written in a way that allows them to be linked and controlled. For example, a change to one requirement may affect other, related requirements, and the methods and tools must support the changes to ensure that errors are caught early and quickly.
texts.text: Your development team must work together to elicit,understand,and document requirements. Often, different team members concentrate on separate aspects of the requirements: the  networking  expert  may  work  on  network  requirements, the  user-interface expert on screens and reports,the database expert on data capture and storage,and so on. Because  the  disparate  requirements  will  be  integrated  into  a  comprehensive whole, requirements must be written in a way that allows them to be linked and controlled. For example, a change to one requirement may affect other, related requirements, and the methods and tools must support the changes to ensure that errors are caught early and quickly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At the same time, the requirements part of your team must work closely with
texts.text: At the same time, the requirements part of your team must work closely with

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Customers and users, so that your team builds a product that serves their needs
texts.text: Customers and users, so that your team builds a product that serves their needs

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Designers,so that they construct a design that fulfills the requirements specification
texts.text: Designers,so that they construct a design that fulfills the requirements specification

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Testers, so that their test scripts adequately evaluate whether the implementation meets the requirements
texts.text: Testers, so that their test scripts adequately evaluate whether the implementation meets the requirements

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Documentation writers, so that they can write user manuals from the specifications
texts.text: Documentation writers, so that they can write user manuals from the specifications

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Your team must also pay attention to measurements that reflect requirements quality. The measures can suggest team activities, such as prototyping some requirements when indicators show that the requirements are not well-understood.
texts.text: Your team must also pay attention to measurements that reflect requirements quality. The measures can suggest team activities, such as prototyping some requirements when indicators show that the requirements are not well-understood.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, you must work as a team to review the requirements definition and specification documents, and to update those documents as the requirements change and grow during the development and maintenance processes.
texts.text: Finally, you must work as a team to review the requirements definition and specification documents, and to update those documents as the requirements change and grow during the development and maintenance processes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.16 WHAT THIS CHAPTER MEANS FOR RESEARCHERS
texts.text: 4.16 WHAT THIS CHAPTER MEANS FOR RESEARCHERS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are many research areas associated with requirements activities. Researchers can
texts.text: There are many research areas associated with requirements activities. Researchers can

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Investigate ways to reduce the amount of uncertainty and risk in requirements
texts.text: Investigate ways to reduce the amount of uncertainty and risk in requirements

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Develop specification techniques and tools that permit easier ways to prove assumptions and assertions,and to demonstrate consistency,completeness,and determinism
texts.text: Develop specification techniques and tools that permit easier ways to prove assumptions and assertions,and to demonstrate consistency,completeness,and determinism

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Develop  tools  to  allow  traceability  across  the  various  intermediate  and  final products of software development.In particular, the tools can assess the impact of a proposed change on products, processes, and resources.
texts.text: Develop  tools  to  allow  traceability  across  the  various  intermediate  and  final products of software development.In particular, the tools can assess the impact of a proposed change on products, processes, and resources.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Evaluate  the  many  different  ways  to  review  requirements: tools, checklists, inspections, walkthroughs, and more. It is important to know which techniques are best for what situations.
texts.text: Evaluate  the  many  different  ways  to  review  requirements: tools, checklists, inspections, walkthroughs, and more. It is important to know which techniques are best for what situations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Create new techniques for simulating requirements behavior.
texts.text: Create new techniques for simulating requirements behavior.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Help us to understand what types of requirements are best for reuse in subsequent projects, and how to write requirements in a way that enhances their later reuse.
texts.text: Help us to understand what types of requirements are best for reuse in subsequent projects, and how to write requirements in a way that enhances their later reuse.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.17 TERM PROJECT
texts.text: 4.17 TERM PROJECT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Your clients  at  FCO  have  prepared  the  following  set  of  English-language  requirements for the Loan Arranger system. Like most sets of requirements, this set must be scrutinized in several ways to determine if it is correct, complete, and consistent. Using the  requirements  here  and  in  supplementary  material  about  the  Loan Arranger  in earlier chapters, evaluate and improve this set of requirements. Use many of the techniques presented in this  chapter, including  requirements  measurement  and Ardis's list. If  necessary, express  the  requirements  in  a  requirements  language  or  modeling technique, to  make  sure  that  the  static  and  dynamic  properties  of  the  system  are expressed well.
texts.text: Your clients  at  FCO  have  prepared  the  following  set  of  English-language  requirements for the Loan Arranger system. Like most sets of requirements, this set must be scrutinized in several ways to determine if it is correct, complete, and consistent. Using the  requirements  here  and  in  supplementary  material  about  the  Loan Arranger  in earlier chapters, evaluate and improve this set of requirements. Use many of the techniques presented in this  chapter, including  requirements  measurement  and Ardis's list. If  necessary, express  the  requirements  in  a  requirements  language  or  modeling technique, to  make  sure  that  the  static  and  dynamic  properties  of  the  system  are expressed well.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Preconditions and Assumptions
texts.text: Preconditions and Assumptions

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The Loan Arranger system assumes that there already exist lenders, borrowers, and loans from which to choose, and that investors exist who are interested in buying bundles of loans.
texts.text: The Loan Arranger system assumes that there already exist lenders, borrowers, and loans from which to choose, and that investors exist who are interested in buying bundles of loans.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The Loan Arranger system contains a repository of information about loans from a variety of lenders.This repository may be empty.
texts.text: The Loan Arranger system contains a repository of information about loans from a variety of lenders.This repository may be empty.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At  regular  intervals, each  lender  provides  reports  listing  the  loans  that  it  has made.Loans that have already been purchased by FCO will be indicated on these reports.
texts.text: At  regular  intervals, each  lender  provides  reports  listing  the  loans  that  it  has made.Loans that have already been purchased by FCO will be indicated on these reports.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Each loan in the Loan Arranger repository represents an investment to then be bundled and sold with other loans.
texts.text: Each loan in the Loan Arranger repository represents an investment to then be bundled and sold with other loans.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The Loan Arranger system may be used by up to four loan analysts simultaneously.
texts.text: The Loan Arranger system may be used by up to four loan analysts simultaneously.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: High-Level Description of Functionality
texts.text: High-Level Description of Functionality

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. The Loan Arranger system will receive monthly reports from each lender of new loans issued by that lender.The loans in the report recently purchased by FCO for its investment portfolio will be marked in the report. The Loan Arranger system will use the report information to update its repository of available loans.
texts.text: The Loan Arranger system will receive monthly reports from each lender of new loans issued by that lender.The loans in the report recently purchased by FCO for its investment portfolio will be marked in the report. The Loan Arranger system will use the report information to update its repository of available loans.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. The Loan Arranger system will receive monthly reports from each lender providing updates about the status of loans issued by that lender.The updated information will include: the current interest rate for an adjustable rate mortgage, and the status of the borrower with respect to the loan (good, late, or default). For loans in the  FCO  portfolio, the  Loan Arranger  will  update  the  data  in  the  repository. Loans not in the FCO portfolio will also be examined in order to determine if a borrower's standing should be updated. FCO will provide each lender with the format for the reports, so that all reports will share a common format.
texts.text: The Loan Arranger system will receive monthly reports from each lender providing updates about the status of loans issued by that lender.The updated information will include: the current interest rate for an adjustable rate mortgage, and the status of the borrower with respect to the loan (good, late, or default). For loans in the  FCO  portfolio, the  Loan Arranger  will  update  the  data  in  the  repository. Loans not in the FCO portfolio will also be examined in order to determine if a borrower's standing should be updated. FCO will provide each lender with the format for the reports, so that all reports will share a common format.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. The loan analyst can change individual data records as described in Data Operations.
texts.text: The loan analyst can change individual data records as described in Data Operations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. All new data must be validated before they are added to the repository (according to the rules described in Data Constraints).
texts.text: All new data must be validated before they are added to the repository (according to the rules described in Data Constraints).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. The loan analyst can use the Loan Arranger to identify bundles of loans to sell to particular investors.
texts.text: The loan analyst can use the Loan Arranger to identify bundles of loans to sell to particular investors.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Chapter 4
texts.text: Chapter 4

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Capturing the Requirements
texts.text: Capturing the Requirements

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Functional Requirements
texts.text: Functional Requirements

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. The loan analyst should be able to review all of the information in the repository for a particular lending institution, a particular loan, or a particular borrower.
texts.text: The loan analyst should be able to review all of the information in the repository for a particular lending institution, a particular loan, or a particular borrower.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. The loan analyst can create, view, edit, or delete a loan from a portfolio or bundle.
texts.text: The loan analyst can create, view, edit, or delete a loan from a portfolio or bundle.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. A loan is added to the portfolio automatically, when the Loan Arranger reads the reports provided by the lenders.A report can be read by the Loan Arranger only after the associated lender has been specified.
texts.text: A loan is added to the portfolio automatically, when the Loan Arranger reads the reports provided by the lenders.A report can be read by the Loan Arranger only after the associated lender has been specified.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. The loan analyst can create a new lender.
texts.text: The loan analyst can create a new lender.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. The loan analyst can delete a lender only if there are no loans in the portfolio associated with this lender.
texts.text: The loan analyst can delete a lender only if there are no loans in the portfolio associated with this lender.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. The loan analyst can change lender contact and phone number but not lender name and identification number.
texts.text: The loan analyst can change lender contact and phone number but not lender name and identification number.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 7. The loan analyst cannot change borrower information.
texts.text: The loan analyst cannot change borrower information.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 8. The loan analyst can ask the system to sort, search, or organize loan information by certain criteria: amount, interest rate, settlement date, borrower, lender, type of loan, or whether it has been marked for inclusion in a certain bundle.The organizational criteria should include ranges, so that information will be included only if it is within two specified bounds (such as between January 1, 2005 and January 1, 2008).The organizational criteria can also be based on exclusion such as all loans not marked, or all loans not between January 1, 2005 and January 1, 2008.
texts.text: The loan analyst can ask the system to sort, search, or organize loan information by certain criteria: amount, interest rate, settlement date, borrower, lender, type of loan, or whether it has been marked for inclusion in a certain bundle.The organizational criteria should include ranges, so that information will be included only if it is within two specified bounds (such as between January 1, 2005 and January 1, 2008).The organizational criteria can also be based on exclusion such as all loans not marked, or all loans not between January 1, 2005 and January 1, 2008.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 9. The loan analyst should be able to request reports in each of three formats: in a file, on the screen, and as a printed report.
texts.text: The loan analyst should be able to request reports in each of three formats: in a file, on the screen, and as a printed report.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 10. The loan analyst should be able to request the following information in a report: any attribute of loan, lender, or borrower, and summary statistics of the attributes (mean, standard deviation, scatter diagram, and histogram).The information in a report can be restricted to a subset of the total information, as described by the loan analyst's organizing criteria.
texts.text: The loan analyst should be able to request the following information in a report: any attribute of loan, lender, or borrower, and summary statistics of the attributes (mean, standard deviation, scatter diagram, and histogram).The information in a report can be restricted to a subset of the total information, as described by the loan analyst's organizing criteria.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 11. The loan analyst must be able to use the Loan Arranger to create bundles that meet the prescribed characteristics of an investment request.The loan analyst can identify these bundles in several ways:
texts.text: The loan analyst must be able to use the Loan Arranger to create bundles that meet the prescribed characteristics of an investment request.The loan analyst can identify these bundles in several ways:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · By manually identifying a subset of loans that must be included in the bundle, either by naming particular loans or by describing them using attributes or ranges
texts.text: By manually identifying a subset of loans that must be included in the bundle, either by naming particular loans or by describing them using attributes or ranges

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · By providing the Loan Arranger with the investment criteria, and allowing the Loan Arranger to run a loan bundle optimization request to select the best set of loans to meet those criteria
texts.text: By providing the Loan Arranger with the investment criteria, and allowing the Loan Arranger to run a loan bundle optimization request to select the best set of loans to meet those criteria

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · By using a combination of the above, where a subset of loans is first chosen (manually or automatically), and then optimizing the chosen subset according to the investment criteria
texts.text: By using a combination of the above, where a subset of loans is first chosen (manually or automatically), and then optimizing the chosen subset according to the investment criteria

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 12. Creating a bundle consists of two steps. First, the loan analyst works with the Loan Arranger to create a bundle according to the criteria as described above. Then the candidate bundle can be accepted, rejected, or modified. Modifying a
texts.text: Creating a bundle consists of two steps. First, the loan analyst works with the Loan Arranger to create a bundle according to the criteria as described above. Then the candidate bundle can be accepted, rejected, or modified. Modifying a

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Section 4.14
texts.text: Section 4.14

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Term Project
texts.text: Term Project

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: bundle means that the analyst may accept some but not all of the loans suggested by  the  Loan Arranger  for  a  bundle, and  can  add  specific  loans  to  the  bundle before accepting it.
texts.text: bundle means that the analyst may accept some but not all of the loans suggested by  the  Loan Arranger  for  a  bundle, and  can  add  specific  loans  to  the  bundle before accepting it.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 13. The loan analyst must be able to mark loans for possible inclusion in a loan bundle. Once a loan is so marked, it is not available for inclusion in any other bundle. If the loan analyst marks a loan and decides not to include it in the bundle, the marking  must  be  removed  and  the  loan  made  available  for  other  bundling decisions.
texts.text: The loan analyst must be able to mark loans for possible inclusion in a loan bundle. Once a loan is so marked, it is not available for inclusion in any other bundle. If the loan analyst marks a loan and decides not to include it in the bundle, the marking  must  be  removed  and  the  loan  made  available  for  other  bundling decisions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 14. When a candidate bundle is accepted, its loans are removed from consideration for use in other bundles.
texts.text: When a candidate bundle is accepted, its loans are removed from consideration for use in other bundles.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 15. All current transactions must be resolved before a loan analyst can exit the Loan Arranger system.
texts.text: All current transactions must be resolved before a loan analyst can exit the Loan Arranger system.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 16. A loan analyst can access a repository of investment requests.This repository may be empty. For each investment request, the analyst uses the request constraints (on risk, profit, and term) to define the parameters of a bundle. Then, the Loan Arranger system identifies loans to be bundled to meet the request constraints.
texts.text: A loan analyst can access a repository of investment requests.This repository may be empty. For each investment request, the analyst uses the request constraints (on risk, profit, and term) to define the parameters of a bundle. Then, the Loan Arranger system identifies loans to be bundled to meet the request constraints.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Data Constraints
texts.text: Data Constraints

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. A single borrower may have more than one loan.
texts.text: A single borrower may have more than one loan.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Every lender must have a unique identifier.
texts.text: Every lender must have a unique identifier.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Every borrower must have a unique identifier.
texts.text: Every borrower must have a unique identifier.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Each loan must have at least one borrower.
texts.text: Each loan must have at least one borrower.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. Each loan must have a loan amount of at least $1000 but not more than $500,000.
texts.text: Each loan must have a loan amount of at least $1000 but not more than $500,000.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. There are two types of loans based on the amount of the loan: regular and jumbo. A regular loan is for any amount less than or equal to $275,000. A jumbo loan is for any amount over $275,000.
texts.text: There are two types of loans based on the amount of the loan: regular and jumbo. A regular loan is for any amount less than or equal to $275,000. A jumbo loan is for any amount over $275,000.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 7. A borrower is considered to be in good standing if all loans to that borrower are in good standing.A borrower is considered to be in default standing if any of the loans to that borrower have default standing. A borrower is said to be in late standing if any of the loans to that borrower have late standing.
texts.text: A borrower is considered to be in good standing if all loans to that borrower are in good standing.A borrower is considered to be in default standing if any of the loans to that borrower have default standing. A borrower is said to be in late standing if any of the loans to that borrower have late standing.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 8. A loan or borrower can change from good to late, from good to default, from late to good, or from late to default. Once a loan or borrower is in default standing, it cannot be changed to another standing.
texts.text: A loan or borrower can change from good to late, from good to default, from late to good, or from late to default. Once a loan or borrower is in default standing, it cannot be changed to another standing.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 9. A loan can change from ARM to FM, and from FM to ARM.
texts.text: A loan can change from ARM to FM, and from FM to ARM.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 10. The profit requested by an investor is a number from 0 to 500. 0 represents no profit on a bundle.A nonzero profit represents the rate of return on the bundle; if the profit is x , then the investor expects to receive the original investment plus x percent of the original investment when the loans are paid off.Thus, if a bundle costs  $1000, and  the  investor  expects  a  rate  of  return  of  40, then  the  investor hopes to have $1400 when all the loans in the bundle are paid off.
texts.text: The profit requested by an investor is a number from 0 to 500. 0 represents no profit on a bundle.A nonzero profit represents the rate of return on the bundle; if the profit is x , then the investor expects to receive the original investment plus x percent of the original investment when the loans are paid off.Thus, if a bundle costs  $1000, and  the  investor  expects  a  rate  of  return  of  40, then  the  investor hopes to have $1400 when all the loans in the bundle are paid off.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 11. No loan can appear in more than one bundle.
texts.text: No loan can appear in more than one bundle.

texts.text: Chapter 4

texts.text: Capturing the Requirements

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Design and Interface Constraints
texts.text: Design and Interface Constraints

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. The Loan Arranger system should work on a Unix system.
texts.text: The Loan Arranger system should work on a Unix system.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. The loan analyst should be able to look at information about more than one loan, lending institution, or borrower at a time.
texts.text: The loan analyst should be able to look at information about more than one loan, lending institution, or borrower at a time.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. The  loan  analyst  must  be  able  to  move  forward  and  backwards  through  the information presented on a screen.When the information is too voluminous to fit on  a  single  screen, the  user  must  be  informed  that  more  information  can  be viewed.
texts.text: The  loan  analyst  must  be  able  to  move  forward  and  backwards  through  the information presented on a screen.When the information is too voluminous to fit on  a  single  screen, the  user  must  be  informed  that  more  information  can  be viewed.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. When the system displays the results of a search, the current organizing criteria must always be displayed along with the information.
texts.text: When the system displays the results of a search, the current organizing criteria must always be displayed along with the information.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. A single record or line of output must never be broken in the middle of a field.
texts.text: A single record or line of output must never be broken in the middle of a field.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. The user must be advised when a search request is inappropriate or illegal.
texts.text: The user must be advised when a search request is inappropriate or illegal.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 7. When an error is encountered, the system should return the user to the previous screen.
texts.text: When an error is encountered, the system should return the user to the previous screen.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Quality Requirements
texts.text: Quality Requirements

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Up to four loan analysts can use the system at a given time.
texts.text: Up to four loan analysts can use the system at a given time.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. If updates are made to any displayed information, the information is refreshed within five seconds of adding, updating, or deleting information.
texts.text: If updates are made to any displayed information, the information is refreshed within five seconds of adding, updating, or deleting information.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. The system must respond to a loan analyst's request for information in less than five seconds from submission of the request.
texts.text: The system must respond to a loan analyst's request for information in less than five seconds from submission of the request.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. The system must be available for use by a loan analyst during 97% of the business day.
texts.text: The system must be available for use by a loan analyst during 97% of the business day.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.18 KEY REFERENCES
texts.text: 4.18 KEY REFERENCES

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Michael Jackson's book Software Requirements and Specifications (1995) provides general advice on how to overcome common problems in understanding and formulating requirements. His ideas can be applied to any requirements technique. Donald Gause and Gerald Weinberg's book Exploring Requirements (1989) focuses on the human side of the requirements process: problems and techniques for working with customers and users and for devising new products.
texts.text: Michael Jackson's book Software Requirements and Specifications (1995) provides general advice on how to overcome common problems in understanding and formulating requirements. His ideas can be applied to any requirements technique. Donald Gause and Gerald Weinberg's book Exploring Requirements (1989) focuses on the human side of the requirements process: problems and techniques for working with customers and users and for devising new products.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  comprehensive  requirements-definition  template  developed  by  James  and Suzanne Robertson can be found at the Web site of the Atlantic Systems Guild: http:// www.systemsguild.com.This template is accompanied by a description of the Volere process model, which is a complete process for eliciting and checking a set of requirements. Use of the template is described in their book Mastering the Requirements Process (1999).
texts.text: A  comprehensive  requirements-definition  template  developed  by  James  and Suzanne Robertson can be found at the Web site of the Atlantic Systems Guild: http:// www.systemsguild.com.This template is accompanied by a description of the Volere process model, which is a complete process for eliciting and checking a set of requirements. Use of the template is described in their book Mastering the Requirements Process (1999).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Peter Coad and Edward Yourdon's book Object-Oriented Analysis (1991) is  a classic text on object-oriented requirements analysis.The most thorough references on the  Unified  Modeling  Language  (UML)  are  the  books  by  James  Rumbaugh, Ivan Jacobson, and  Grady  Booch, especially  the Unified  Modeling  Language  Reference Manual , and the documents released by the Object Management Group; the latter can

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Beginning in 1993, the IEEE Computer Society has started to sponsor two conferences that were directly related to requirements and were held in alternate years: the  International  Conference  on  Requirements  Engineering  and  the  International Symposium on Requirements Engineering.These conferences merged in 2002 to form the  International  Requirements  Engineering  Conference, which  is  held  every  year. Information  about  upcoming  conferences  and  about  proceedings  from  past  conferences can be found at the Computer Society's Web page: http://www.computer.org.
texts.text: Beginning in 1993, the IEEE Computer Society has started to sponsor two conferences that were directly related to requirements and were held in alternate years: the  International  Conference  on  Requirements  Engineering  and  the  International Symposium on Requirements Engineering.These conferences merged in 2002 to form the  International  Requirements  Engineering  Conference, which  is  held  every  year. Information  about  upcoming  conferences  and  about  proceedings  from  past  conferences can be found at the Computer Society's Web page: http://www.computer.org.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Requirements Engineering Journal focuses exclusively on new results in eliciting, representing, and validating requirements, mostly with respect to software systems. IEEE Software had special issues on requirements engineering in March 1994, March 1996, March/April 1998, May/June 2000, January/February 2003, and March/April 2004. Other IEEE publications often have special issues on particular types of requirements analysis and specification methods. For example, the September 1990 issues of IEEE Computer, IEEE Software , and IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering focused on formal methods, as did the May 1997 and January 1998 issues of IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering and the April 1996 issue of IEEE Computer.
texts.text: The Requirements Engineering Journal focuses exclusively on new results in eliciting, representing, and validating requirements, mostly with respect to software systems. IEEE Software had special issues on requirements engineering in March 1994, March 1996, March/April 1998, May/June 2000, January/February 2003, and March/April 2004. Other IEEE publications often have special issues on particular types of requirements analysis and specification methods. For example, the September 1990 issues of IEEE Computer, IEEE Software , and IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering focused on formal methods, as did the May 1997 and January 1998 issues of IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering and the April 1996 issue of IEEE Computer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are several standards related to software requirements. The U.S. Department  of  Defense  has  produced  MilStd-498, Data  Item  Description  for  Software Requirements  Specifications  (SRS). The  IEEE  has  produced  IEEE  Std  830-1998, which is a set of recommended practices and standards for formulating and structuring requirements specifications.
texts.text: There are several standards related to software requirements. The U.S. Department  of  Defense  has  produced  MilStd-498, Data  Item  Description  for  Software Requirements  Specifications  (SRS). The  IEEE  has  produced  IEEE  Std  830-1998, which is a set of recommended practices and standards for formulating and structuring requirements specifications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There  are  several  tools  that  support  requirements  capture  and  traceability. DOORS/ERS  (Telelogic), Analyst  Pro  (Goda  Software), and  RequisitePro  (IBM Rational) are popular tools for managing requirements, tracing requirements in downstream artifacts, tracking changes, and assessing the impact of changes. Most modeling notations have tool support that at the least supports the creation and editing of models, usually supports some form of well-formedness checking and report generation, and at the best offers automated validation and verification.An independent survey of requirements tools is located at www.systemsguild.com.
texts.text: There  are  several  tools  that  support  requirements  capture  and  traceability. DOORS/ERS  (Telelogic), Analyst  Pro  (Goda  Software), and  RequisitePro  (IBM Rational) are popular tools for managing requirements, tracing requirements in downstream artifacts, tracking changes, and assessing the impact of changes. Most modeling notations have tool support that at the least supports the creation and editing of models, usually supports some form of well-formedness checking and report generation, and at the best offers automated validation and verification.An independent survey of requirements tools is located at www.systemsguild.com.

texts.orig: 4.19 EXERCISES

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Developers work together with customers and users to define requirements and specify what the proposed system will do. If, once it is built, the system works according to specification but harms someone physically or financially, who is responsible?
texts.text: Developers work together with customers and users to define requirements and specify what the proposed system will do. If, once it is built, the system works according to specification but harms someone physically or financially, who is responsible?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Among the many nonfunctional requirements that can be included in a specification are those related to safety and reliability. How can we ensure that these requirements are testable, in the sense defined by the Robertsons? In particular, how can we demonstrate the reliability of a system that is required never to fail?
texts.text: Among the many nonfunctional requirements that can be included in a specification are those related to safety and reliability. How can we ensure that these requirements are testable, in the sense defined by the Robertsons? In particular, how can we demonstrate the reliability of a system that is required never to fail?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. If a decision table has two identical columns, then the requirements specification is redundant. How can we tell if the specification is contradictory? What other characteristics of a decision table warn us of problems with the requirements?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. Write a Parnas table that describes the output of the algorithm for finding the roots of a quadratic equation using the quadratic formula.
texts.text: Write a Parnas table that describes the output of the algorithm for finding the roots of a quadratic equation using the quadratic formula.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 7. Write a state-machine specification to illustrate the requirements of an automatic banking machine (ABM).
texts.text: Write a state-machine specification to illustrate the requirements of an automatic banking machine (ABM).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 8. A state-machine specification is complete if and only if there is a transition specified for every possible combination of state and input symbol.We can change an incomplete specification to a complete one by adding an extra state, called a trap state. Once a transition is made to the trap state, the system remains in the trap state, no matter the input. For example, if 0, 1, and 2 are the only possible inputs, the system depicted by Figure 4.33 can be completed by adding a trap state as shown in Figure 4.34. In same manner, complete your state-machine specification from Exercise 7.
texts.text: A state-machine specification is complete if and only if there is a transition specified for every possible combination of state and input symbol.We can change an incomplete specification to a complete one by adding an extra state, called a trap state. Once a transition is made to the trap state, the system remains in the trap state, no matter the input. For example, if 0, 1, and 2 are the only possible inputs, the system depicted by Figure 4.33 can be completed by adding a trap state as shown in Figure 4.34. In same manner, complete your state-machine specification from Exercise 7.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 9. A safety property is an invariant property that specifies that a particular bad behavior never happens; for  example, a  safety  property  of  the  turnstile  problem  is  that  the  number  of entries into the zoo is never more than the number of entry fees paid.A liveness property is a property that specifies that a particular behavior eventually happens; for example, a liveness  property  for  the  turnstile  problem  is  that  when  an  entry  fee  is  paid, the  turnstile becomes unlocked. Similarly, a liveness property for the library system is that every borrow

texts.orig: 11. Sometimes part of a system may be built quickly to demonstrate feasibility or functionality  to  a  customer. This prototype system is usually incomplete; the real system is constructed  after  the  customer  and  developer  evaluate  the  prototype. Should  the  system requirements document be written before or after a prototype is developed? Why?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 12. Write a set of UML models (use-case diagram, MSC diagrams, class diagram) for an online telephone directory to replace the phonebook that is provided to you by your phone company.The directory should be able to provide phone numbers when presented with a name; it should also list area codes for different parts of the country and generate emergency telephone numbers for your area.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 17. Write a Z specification for a presentation scheduling system.The system keeps a record of which presenters are to give presentations on which dates. No presenter should be scheduled to give more than one presentation. No more than four presentations should be scheduled for any particular date. There should be operations to Add and Remove presentations from the schedule, to Swap the dates of two presentations, to List the presentations scheduled for a particular date, to List the date on which a particular presenter is scheduled to speak, and to send a Reminder message to each presenter on the date of his or  her  presentation. You  may  define  any  additional  operations  that  help  simplify  the specification.
texts.text: Write a Z specification for a presentation scheduling system.The system keeps a record of which presenters are to give presentations on which dates. No presenter should be scheduled to give more than one presentation. No more than four presentations should be scheduled for any particular date. There should be operations to Add and Remove presentations from the schedule, to Swap the dates of two presentations, to List the presentations scheduled for a particular date, to List the date on which a particular presenter is scheduled to speak, and to send a Reminder message to each presenter on the date of his or  her  presentation. You  may  define  any  additional  operations  that  help  simplify  the specification.

In particular, write axioms for nongenerator operations unreserve, isOnLoan , and isOnReserve . Modify your axioms for operation unreserve so  that  this  operation assumes that multiple requests to put an item on reserve might occur between two requests to unreserve that item.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 19. What kinds of problems should you look for when doing a requirements review? Make a checklist of these problems. Can the checklist be universally applicable or is it better to use a checklist that is specific to the application domain?
texts.text: What kinds of problems should you look for when doing a requirements review? Make a checklist of these problems. Can the checklist be universally applicable or is it better to use a checklist that is specific to the application domain?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 20. Is  it  ever  possible  to  have  the  requirements  definition  document  be  the  same  as  the requirements specification? What are the pros and cons of having two documents?
texts.text: Is  it  ever  possible  to  have  the  requirements  definition  document  be  the  same  as  the requirements specification? What are the pros and cons of having two documents?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 21. Pfleeger and Hatton (1997) examined the quality of a system that had been specified using formal methods.They found that the system was unusually well-structured and easy to test.They speculated that the high quality was due to the thoroughness of the specification, not necessarily its formality. How could you design a study to determine whether it is formality or thoroughness that leads to high quality?
texts.text: Pfleeger and Hatton (1997) examined the quality of a system that had been specified using formal methods.They found that the system was unusually well-structured and easy to test.They speculated that the high quality was due to the thoroughness of the specification, not necessarily its formality. How could you design a study to determine whether it is formality or thoroughness that leads to high quality?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 22. Sometimes a customer requests a requirement that you know is impossible to implement. Should you agree to put the requirement in the definition and specification documents anyway, thinking that you might come up with a novel way of meeting it, or thinking that you will ask that the requirement be dropped later? Discuss the ethical implications of promising what you know you cannot deliver.
texts.text: Sometimes a customer requests a requirement that you know is impossible to implement. Should you agree to put the requirement in the definition and specification documents anyway, thinking that you might come up with a novel way of meeting it, or thinking that you will ask that the requirement be dropped later? Discuss the ethical implications of promising what you know you cannot deliver.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 23. Find a set of natural-language requirements at your job or at this book's Web site. Review the requirements to determine if there are any problems. For example, are they consistent? Ambiguous? Conflicting? Do they contain any design or implementation decisions? Which representation techniques might help reveal and eliminate these problems? If the problems remain in the requirements, what is their likely impact as the system is designed and implemented?

texts.text: software architecture documentation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the last chapter, we learned how to work with our customers to determine what they want the proposed system to do. The result of the requirements process was two documents:a requirements document that captures the customers' needs and a requirements specification that describes how the proposed system should behave. The next step in development is to start designing how the system will be constructed. If we are building a relatively small system, we may be able to progress directly from the specification to the design of data structures and algorithms. However,if we are building a larger system, then we will want to decompose the system into units of manageable size, such as subsystems or modules, before we contemplate details about the data or code.
texts.text: In the last chapter, we learned how to work with our customers to determine what they want the proposed system to do. The result of the requirements process was two documents:a requirements document that captures the customers' needs and a requirements specification that describes how the proposed system should behave. The next step in development is to start designing how the system will be constructed. If we are building a relatively small system, we may be able to progress directly from the specification to the design of data structures and algorithms. However,if we are building a larger system, then we will want to decompose the system into units of manageable size, such as subsystems or modules, before we contemplate details about the data or code.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The software architecture is this decomposition. In this chapter, we examine different types of decomposition. Just as buildings are sometimes constructed of prefabricated sections based on commonly needed architectural constructs, some prefabricated software architectural styles can be used as guidelines for decomposing a new system. Often,there will be multiple ways to design the architecture, so we explore how to compare competing designs and choose the one that best suits our needs. We learn how to document our decisions in a software architecture document (SAD) as the architecture starts  to  stabilize, and  how  to  verify  that  this  architecture  will  meet  the  customer's requirements.The steps we lay out result in a software architecture that guides the rest of the system's development.
texts.text: The software architecture is this decomposition. In this chapter, we examine different types of decomposition. Just as buildings are sometimes constructed of prefabricated sections based on commonly needed architectural constructs, some prefabricated software architectural styles can be used as guidelines for decomposing a new system. Often,there will be multiple ways to design the architecture, so we explore how to compare competing designs and choose the one that best suits our needs. We learn how to document our decisions in a software architecture document (SAD) as the architecture starts  to  stabilize, and  how  to  verify  that  this  architecture  will  meet  the  customer's requirements.The steps we lay out result in a software architecture that guides the rest of the system's development.
